title: Psalms 143
subtitle: Hear My Prayer, O LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “No One Living Is Righteous Before You”

heb.paragraph: "Do not bring your servant into judgment, for no one living is righteous (ṣaddîq) before you" (v.2). The SEVENTH and final penitential psalm (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). David appeals NOT to his own righteousness but to God’s faithfulness (v.1). No one is righteous before God — the same theology as Psalm 14:3 and Romans 3:10. His spirit grows faint, his heart dismayed (v.4). He remembers God’s former deeds, meditates, reaches out like parched land (vv.5–6).

notes.note: "Hear my prayer. In your faithfulness and righteousness come to my relief. Do not bring your servant into judgment, for no one living is righteous before you." The appeal is to GOD’s righteousness, not David’s. Universal sinfulness acknowledged.

"I remember the days of long ago; I meditate on all your works. I spread out my hands to you; I thirst for you like a parched land." Memory, meditation, outstretched hands, thirst: four postures of desperate seeking.

refs.note: No one will be declared righteous by the works of the law—echoing verse 2.
A person is not justified by works of the law but by faith in Christ.
How can mere mortals prove their innocence before God?

hist.context: Psalm 143 is the seventh and final Penitential Psalm in church tradition. David's plea for mercy acknowledges that no living person is righteous before God (v2)—a theological foundation for justification by grace. The enemy has crushed him, making him dwell in darkness. The meditation on God's past works provides comfort: remembering is the antidote to despair.

sections.header: Verses 7–12 — “Teach Me to Do Your Will”

heb.paragraph: "Let your good Spirit (rūaḥăk̅ā) lead me on level ground" (v.10). Seven requests in rapid succession: answer quickly (v.7), show love in the morning (v.8), rescue from enemies (v.9), teach me to do your will (v.10), let your good Spirit lead me (v.10), preserve my life (v.11), silence my enemies (v.12). "Teach me to do your will, for you are my God" (v.10) — the request that summarises all penitential psalms: not just forgiveness but transformation.

notes.note: "Let the morning bring me word of your unfailing love. Show me the way I should go, for to you I entrust my life." Morning ḥesed: the first light brings the first word from God. Direction flows from entrustment.

"Teach me to do your will, for you are my God; may your good Spirit lead me on level ground." The final penitential psalm’s request: not just pardon but obedience. Not just mercy but transformation. The Spirit as guide on level ground.

refs.note: The Spirit of truth will guide you into all truth—divine leading.
Show me your ways, LORD; teach me your paths; guide me in your truth.
Those who are led by the Spirit of God are the children of God.

hist.context: David's request for guidance—'teach me to do your will... may your good Spirit lead me on level ground'—anticipates NT language of the Spirit's leading. The psalm's conclusion prays for deliverance as God's servant: 'in your unfailing love, silence my enemies.' The final Davidic psalms (142-145) consistently end with this confidence: the covenant servant will be delivered; the wicked will be destroyed.

ppl.text: " The appeal is to GOD’s righteousness, not David’s…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " Morning ḥesed: the first light brings the first word from God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe enemy pursues me, he crushes me to the ground; he makes me dwell in the darkness like those long dead.ESVFor the enemy has pursued my soul; he has crushed my life to the ground; he has made me sit in darkness like those long dead.NIVI spread out my hands to you; I thirst for you like a parched land.ESVI stretch out my hands to you; my soul thirsts for you like a parched land. Selah

rows.label: No One Righteous — 143:1–6
rows.text: Seventh penitential psalm. "NO ONE living is righteous before you." Parched-land thirst.

hebtext.note: "Do not bring your servant into judgment, for no one living is righteous (ṣaddîq) before you" (v.2). The SEVENTH and final penitential psalm (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). David appeals NOT to his own righteousness but to God’s faithfulness (v.1). No one is righteous before God — the same theology as Psalm 14:3 and Romans 3:10. His spirit grows faint, his heart dismayed (v.4). He remembers God’s former deeds, meditates, reaches out like parched land (vv.5–6).

"Let your good Spirit (rūaḥăk̅ā) lead me on level ground" (v.10). Seven requests in rapid succession: answer quickly (v.7), show love in the morning (v.8), rescue from enemies (v.9), teach me to do your will (v.10), let your good Spirit lead me (v.10), preserve my life (v.11), silence my enemies (v.12). "Teach me to do your will, for you are my God" (v.10) — the request that summarises all penitential psalms: not just forgiveness but transformation.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: the seventh and final penitential psalm. Holiness: universal sinfulness + Spirit-led obedience. The seven penitential psalms close with transformation theology.

prayer_prompt: Hear my prayer, LORD; in your faithfulness answer me. Consider asking God about the prayers that feel unanswered — and what faithfulness you're trusting.
